companyCaseStudy.title: How LinkedIn Used Kafka & Samza to Move 1+ Trillion Events/Day from Bottlenecked DBs to a Stream Pipeline
companyCaseStudy.summary: LinkedIn went from a monolithic activity pipeline that overloaded databases during spikes to a distributed streaming backbone that processed over 1 trillion events per day and sustained peak throughputs on the order of 1M messages/sec. The result: decoupled producers/consumers, orders-of-magnitude better throughput, and real-time features like feeds and alerting.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook

In the mid-2010s LinkedIn faced a classic scale problem: the social graph and activity systems were bursting at the seams. The engineering team moved critical signals onto Kafka and stream processors (Samza), enabling a system that today handles over 1 trillion messages per day and sustained peaks in the ~1M messages/sec range — turning a blocking database pipeline into a horizontally-scalable stream bus.

## Problem

LinkedIn's old architecture was simple but brittle: services wrote directly to durable databases (MySQL and other stores) and downstream jobs read from the DB. That produced:

- Tight coupling between producers and consumers (schema and availability issues)
- Write amplification and hot spots on the DB during bursts (feature rollouts, viral content)
- High end-to-end latency for features that needed near-real-time updates (feeds, notifications, alerts)

Concrete scale pain: production traffic grew to hundreds of millions of events per day and spiky campaigns caused sustained DB overloads.

## Challenge

The constraints were clear:

- Keep data durable and replayable for reprocessing and debugging
- Support many heterogeneous consumers (analytics, personalization, real-time features)
- Provide low-latency delivery for features that need events in seconds, not hours
- Operate reliably at massive scale with predictable operational surface area

## Solution — Build a Streaming Backbone (Kafka + Samza + Storage)

LinkedIn designed a streaming-first data plane:

1. Producers publish structured events to Kafka topics instead of writing directly to operational DBs.
2. Kafka brokers provide durable, replicated, partitioned logs — enabling replay and decoupling.
3. Samza (stream processor) consumes Kafka topics for real-time processing (aggregation, joins, enrichment) and writes outputs to downstream stores or materialized views.
4. Consumers (features, analytics systems) read either directly from Kafka or from materialized stores produced by Samza.

Why this worked:

- Kafka's append-only log and partitioning gave both throughput and durable replay.
- Samza's local state and partition affinity enabled fault-tolerant, low-latency processing that scales with partitions.
- Decoupling meant producers never blocked on consumer slowness; backpressure became an operational choice (retention, producer throttling), not an accident of DB overload.

### What changed architecturally

```mermaid
graph LR
  Producers[Microservices / Mobile / Web] -->|Publish events| Kafka[Kafka Cluster (Partitioned Logs)]
  Kafka --> Samza[Samza Stream Processors]
  Samza --> StateDB[Materialized Stores (Cassandra / RocksDB)]
  Samza --> Analytics[OLAP / Offline Systems]
  Kafka --> Consumers[Low-latency Consumers: Feed Service, Notification Service]
  Producers --> AuditDB[Operational DBs (reduced load)]
```

This allowed multiple consumers to consume the same events independently and reprocess historical windows by seeking in the Kafka log.

## Key engineering decisions (and why)

- Use partitioning keys aligned to access patterns (memberId, messageId) so related events are processed by the same partition and Samza task — keeps state local and reduces cross-shard coordination.
- Tune retention to balance replayability vs storage costs. For many core signals LinkedIn kept multi-day retention to allow reprocessing of pipelines after code fixes.
- Optimize producer batching and compression (snappy) to maximize throughput and reduce broker network I/O.
- Use replication factor (e.g., RF=3) to ensure availability during broker failures.
- Push computation to stream processors near the data (Samza tasks colocated with Kafka consumers) to reduce data movement.

## Before / After (high-level)

| Metric | Before (DB-centric) | After (Kafka + Samza) |
|--------|---------------------:|----------------------:|
| Daily events | Hundreds of millions | >1 trillion events/day |
| Peak throughput | Spiky, DB-limited | Sustained peaks ~1M msgs/sec |
| Producer coupling | Producers blocked on downstream | Producers append-only (non-blocking) |
| Replayability | Hard (backfills expensive) | Built-in (seek & replay) |
| Time-to-value for real-time features | Minutes–hours | Seconds–minutes |

## Results & impact

- Throughput: The streaming backbone handled event volumes that previously would have overloaded DBs; LinkedIn publicly reported Kafka usage in the scale of trillions of messages per day.
- Feature velocity: New real-time features (feed updates, messaging, notification delivery) could subscribe to the same event stream and be rolled out faster without coordination scars.
- Operational simplicity: Instead of bespoke replication & change-data-capture plumbing, Kafka provided a unified log that simplified end-to-end visibility and troubleshooting.
- Durability + Replay: Bugs in downstream code could be fixed and re-run over historical data by seeking Kafka offsets — avoiding expensive DB exports/imports.

## Tradeoffs and caveats

- Operational overhead: Running large Kafka clusters, Zookeeper, and Samza clusters added operational complexity and required new SRE practices.
- Schema evolution discipline: With many consumers, schema changes needed careful compatibility management (Avro + schema registry patterns became important).
- Retention/storage costs: Keeping multi-day retention for high-throughput topics required careful tiering and offload strategies.

## Practical paraphrased recipe (what you can copy)

- Start by moving high-volume, fan-out events to a partitioned log (Kafka).
- Choose partition keys that match your stateful processing to maximize locality.
- Implement stream processors colocated with consumers (Samza or similar) and use local RocksDB/State stores for joins/aggregates.
- Keep retention long enough to allow safe reprocessing, and add an archived cold storage tier for older data.
- Automate producer batching and compression to extract network and broker efficiency.

## Visualization — flow of an event (simplified)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[User action: Like / Share / Message] --> B[Producer service]
  B --> C[Kafka topic (partitioned)]
  C --> D[Samza processor (enrichment)]
  D --> E[Materialized store: feed shard]
  E --> F[Feed service returns update to user]
```

## Lessons learned

- Decouple: Logs are a universal decoupling primitive. They turn transient spikes into durable traffic that can be shaped.
- Locality matters: Partition keys that co-locate related events massively simplify stateful processing and reduce cross-machine coordination.
- Operationalize schema & retention: Streams are great — but without schema governance and retention strategy they'll become a liability.
keyTakeaways: Move high-volume, fan-out events to a partitioned append-only log (Kafka) to decouple producers and consumers — LinkedIn handled >1 trillion events/day after doing this.
Colocate stateful processors (Samza) with partition affinity and local state (RocksDB) to get low-latency, fault-tolerant processing without cross-shard coordination.
Invest in schema management and retention/offload policies: replayability is powerful but needs governance and storage planning.

concept.title: When Machines Agree: Raft Consensus Explained with Examples
concept.summary: Distributed systems need a single source of truth even when machines fail or messages are lost. Raft is a readable, practical consensus algorithm for leader election and replicated logs that helps clusters agree on the next actions to perform.
concept.explanation:
## Why it matters

Imagine a bank ledger replicated across multiple machines. If servers disagree about the order of transactions, customers could see inconsistent balances, duplicate payments, or lost updates. Consensus algorithms solve the problem of making a set of unreliable machines agree on a single sequence of operations so the system behaves as if there were one fault-tolerant state machine.

Raft is one of the most practical consensus algorithms used in production. It gives you a clear mental model: one leader accepts client requests, replicates them to followers, and once a majority acknowledges, the command is committed and applied.

---

## Analogy: A team writing a single official book

- Think of the cluster as a team writing a shared book. If everyone edits their own copy, the book diverges.
- Raft picks a single editor in chief, the leader. Everyone submits proposed paragraphs to the editor in chief. The editor proposes the paragraph to the team, and once a majority signs off, the paragraph becomes part of the official book.
- If the editor disappears, the team elects a new editor and continues from the latest agreed paragraph.

This analogy highlights three roles of Raft: leader, followers, and candidates during elections.

---

## High level flow (sequence diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  Client->>Leader: Append command
  Leader->>Follower1: AppendEntries(term, prevIndex, entries)
  Leader->>Follower2: AppendEntries(...)
  Follower1-->>Leader: Ack
  Follower2-->>Leader: Ack
  Note right of Leader: If majority acks -> commit
  Leader-->>Client: Success
```

---

## Core concepts (short)

- **Terms**: logical epochs. Each election increases the term. Terms prevent stale leaders from staying in control.
- **Leader election**: nodes become candidates and request votes. The node with majority votes becomes leader for the current term.
- **Log replication**: leader appends entries to its log and asks followers to append them via AppendEntries RPCs. When a majority acknowledges an entry at a given index, the leader marks it committed.
- **Safety invariant**: a leader must contain all committed entries from previous terms before it can commit new entries. This ensures the replicated log never loses committed entries.

---

## Before / After adopting Raft

| Situation | Before Raft (no consensus) | After Raft (with Raft) |
|----------|---------------------------|------------------------|
| Command ordering | Different nodes may accept commands in different order -> divergence | Single leader orders commands -> consistent sequence across majority
| Fault tolerance | Losing one node can make state unreliable or diverge | Can tolerate f failures out of 2f+1 nodes and still agree
| Reasoning about correctness | Hard, ad hoc coordination, many corner cases | Formalized safety and liveness properties, easier to reason about
| Client interaction | Client may need to talk to many nodes with retries | Client talks to leader; leader forwards or redirects

---

## When to use Raft

- You need a strongly consistent, replicated log or key-value store (examples: configuration stores, metadata services, leader election for higher-level services).
- You require linearizable operations: once a command returns success, all future reads must see its effect.
- The cluster is relatively small (common production sizes: 3, 5, 7 nodes). Raft is not meant for massively scaled consensus groups.

Avoid using Raft if you need eventual consistency only, need thousands of nodes participating in consensus, or can accept relaxed guarantees for higher throughput.

---

## Common pitfalls and how to avoid them

- *Assuming leader is always stable*: Leaders can step down due to network partitions or crashes. Implement client retry and redirect logic (clients should retry other nodes or use a DNS entry for leader address).
- *Ignoring configuration changes*: Changing the membership of the cluster is tricky. Use Raft's joint-consensus approach (two-phase membership change) to avoid losing majority.
- *Thinking more replicas always helps*: Increasing nodes increases quorum size and commit latency. For global distribution, consider hierarchical approaches (local consensus per region + cross-region replication) instead of a single large Raft cluster.
- *Blocking long-running commands in log*: Keep log entries small and idempotent. Offload heavy work; the log should represent durable intent, not long-running execution.
- *State machine non-determinism*: All nodes must apply commands deterministically. Non-deterministic behavior breaks correctness.

---

## Implementation sketch and example

The key RPCs are RequestVote and AppendEntries. Below is a simplified pseudo Go handler for AppendEntries that shows the core checks.

```go
// simplified AppendEntries handler
func AppendEntries(args AppendArgs) AppendReply {
  if args.Term < currentTerm { return Reply{Term: currentTerm, Success:false} }
  // become follower if seeing higher term
  currentTerm = args.Term
  leaderId = args.LeaderId

  // consistency check: does prevLogIndex match and term match?
  if !logMatches(args.PrevLogIndex, args.PrevLogTerm) {
    return Reply{Term: currentTerm, Success:false}
  }

  // append any new entries
  appendToLog(args.Entries)

  // update commit index
  if args.LeaderCommit > commitIndex {
    commitIndex = min(args.LeaderCommit, lastLogIndex())
    applyCommittedEntries()
  }
  return Reply{Term: currentTerm, Success:true}
}
```

This snippet hides many details (snapshotting, log compaction, election timers, leader heartbeats), but captures the core idea: check term, enforce log consistency via prevLogIndex/prevLogTerm, append, and update commit index.

---

## Visual: leader election and log flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  subgraph Cluster
    A[Node A] --- B[Node B]
    B --- C[Node C]
    C --- A
  end
  client(Client) -->|write| A
  A -->|is leader| B
  A -->|is leader| C
  B -->|ack| A
  C -->|ack| A
  A -->|commit| client
```

---

## Debugging tips

- Monitor election timeouts and leader changes; frequent elections often point to network instability or misconfigured timers.
- Track commit latency (time between append and commit) and the slowest follower; slow followers increase latency.
- Use snapshots and compaction to prevent unbounded log growth; watch for snapshotting stalls.

---

## TL;DR

Raft makes replicated state machines understandable by selecting a leader to order commands and using majority quorums to commit them. Use it for small-to-medium sized clusters that need strong consistency. Pay attention to election timing, membership changes, and deterministic application of log entries.
keyTakeaways: Raft provides leader-based consensus: a leader orders commands, followers replicate, majority quorums commit.
Use Raft when you need strong consistency and a small, fault-tolerant replicated log; avoid it for huge clusters or when eventual consistency suffices.
Watch out for election tuning, membership changes, non-deterministic state machines, and unbounded logs.
